file_name: Parvin et al. - 2026 - Computationally efficient explainable AI framework for skin cancer detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed a computationally efficient explainable AI framework for skin cancer detection using a hybrid approach combining Deep Learning (Convolutional Neural Networks like MobileNetV2, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet201, Xception) and Machine Learning (Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, etc.) with optimization techniques (BFO, PCA, LDA). The best performing model was MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR, achieving 99.33% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques including Grad-CAM++ and LIME were also employed to validate interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a 'computationally efficient explainable AI framework' for skin cancer detection. It details specific methodologies including the use of five CNN models (MobileNetV2, ResNet50, etc.), seven ML models, optimization techniques (BFO, PCA, LDA), and XAI methods (Grad-CAM++, LIME). It reports original quantitative results such as 99.33% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset and computational metrics (35.1 ± 1.24 seconds), distinguishing it from a review paper which would summarize prior literature.

title: Computationally Efficient Explainable AI Framework for Skin Cancer Detection
journal: Advances in Science, Technology and Engineering Systems Journal
brief_summary: This study developed a computationally efficient and explainable AI framework for skin cancer detection to address the limitations of conventional diagnostic methods. The proposed hybrid model combines five pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (MobileNetV2, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet201, Xception) with seven Machine Learning classifiers and optimization techniques like Bacterial Foraging Optimization, PCA, and LDA. The framework was evaluated on a balanced dataset of 12,450 dermoscopic images sourced from Kaggle, covering five classes including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. The MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR configuration achieved the highest performance with 99.33% accuracy, 99.47% precision, and 97.33% recall, while maintaining a low inference time of 35.1 ± 1.24 seconds. Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM++ and LIME, were employed to validate model interpretability by highlighting clinically relevant lesion areas. The results demonstrate that the framework offers a robust, transparent, and computationally viable solution for automated skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Class 0 in the dataset, with 2,958 samples.
Class 1 in the dataset, with 2,093 samples.

Class 2 in the dataset, with 3,000 samples. Note: The paper uses this as a distinct class label in the dataset description (Table 2) and confusion matrix, though it is a broad category often encompassing melanoma and SCC in other contexts.
Class 3 in the dataset, with 2,863 samples.
Class 4 in the dataset, with 2,536 samples.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin cancer (Non-Cancerous, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Malignant Lesions, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer types using a hybrid framework of CNNs and ML classifiers. The target variable consists of five discrete classes (labeled 0-4). Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A balanced dataset of dermoscopic and clinical skin images collected from a secondary data resource platform called Kaggle. The dataset was carefully examined and annotated by a professional oncologist, with each image defined by the type and stage of lesion.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid framework (CNNs and ML classifiers) after being split into 80% training and 20% testing sets.
use.validation: Used for validation via 10-fold cross-validation on the MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR model.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance (20% of the dataset).
use.other: Used for preprocessing analysis (filtering methods) and XAI validation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the consistency and generalization performance of the MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR model across various data partitions.
To demonstrate the stability of the model by calculating the average standard deviation values for Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score across the 10 folds.
To quantitatively evaluate the quality of image filtering methods (e.g., Bilateral Filter) in removing noise while maintaining structural information.
To quantitatively evaluate the quality of image filtering methods, indicating the ratio between the maximum possible power of a signal and the power of distorting noise.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and standard deviation across 10-fold cross-validation, but no statistical significance testing between models was reported.

filtering_performance.metric: MSE and PSNR for Bilateral Filter
filtering_performance.notes: Bilateral Filter was identified as the best pre-processing method with lowest MSE and maximum PSNR across all classes.

HAM10000 (via Kaggle).notes: The MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR configuration achieved the highest performance. XAI techniques (Grad-CAM++ and LIME) were used to validate interpretability.

architecture_used.main_model: MobileNetV2 (best performing), ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet201, Xception
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (specific weights not detailed, but described as 'pre-trained')

architecture_details.backbone: MobileNetV2 (identified as lightweight and resource-efficient with 3.505M parameters, 327.487M MACS, 654.973M FLOPS)

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (standard ML classifiers used)
training_details.training_schedule: 80% training, 20% testing split; 10-fold cross-validation used for validation

classification_or_output_head.type: Hybrid Deep Learning and Machine Learning

approximate_parameters.DenseNet201: 498.6 million (inferred from '498 600' in Table 4 context of parameters)

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for contrast enhancement and noise reduction
Gaussian filtering for noise removal while preserving edges
Median filtering for eliminating noise impulses while preserving sharp edges
Bilateral filtering for edge-preserving nonlinear noise reduction
Min filtering (minimum entropy filtering) for eliminating bright outlier noise (salt noise)
Max filtering for eliminating dark outlier noise (pepper noise)
Average (mean) filtering for smoothing intensity changes
Handling of missing or corrupted images via quality analysis and reconstruction methods
conclusions: The proposed hybrid framework combining MobileNetV2, Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), and Logistic Regression (LR) achieved the highest performance with 99.33% accuracy, 99.47% precision, 97.33% recall, and 98.31% F1-score on the HAM10000 dataset.

The MobileNetV2 + LDA + LR model demonstrated superior computational efficiency with a time complexity of 35.1 ± 1.24 seconds and moderate memory usage (PM: 2548.77 MiB, INC: 1160.05 MiB), making it suitable for resource-restricted clinical environments.

Bilateral filtering was identified as the most effective pre-processing technique, yielding the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE) and highest Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) across all lesion classes compared to other filters like Gaussian, Median, and Average.

Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM++ and LIME, successfully validated the model's interpretability by highlighting clinically relevant lesion areas, thereby increasing transparency and credibility.
MobileNetV2 proved to be the most effective backbone for feature extraction among the tested CNNs (ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet201, Xception), particularly when optimized with LDA.

LDA optimization significantly improved classification performance compared to unoptimized configurations and other optimization techniques like Bacterial Foraging Optimization (BFO) or Principal Component Analysis (PCA).

The framework addresses limitations of conventional diagnosis by offering a cost-effective, automated, and reliable alternative to resource-consuming and inter-observer variable methods like dermoscopy and histopathology.

Other configurations, such as ResNet50 + SVC and DenseNet201 + BFO + LR, achieved lower accuracies (89.07% and 77.75% respectively) and higher computational requirements, confirming the trade-off advantage of the proposed MobileNetV2-based model.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset sourced from Kaggle, lacking external validation on independent cohorts or diverse clinical settings, which limits the assessment of generalizability and robustness.

The dataset contains a class labeled 'Malignant lesions' (Class 2) which is a broad category distinct from 'Melanoma' (Class 3) and 'Squamous Cell Carcinoma' (Class 4), potentially obscuring specific diagnostic capabilities for distinct malignant subtypes.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or statistical significance tests were reported; performance stability was assessed only via standard deviation across 10-fold cross-validation.

The paper claims the framework is suitable for 'resource-restricted clinical environments' based on inference time (35.1 ± 1.24 seconds) and memory usage, but does not provide evidence of deployment in real-world clinical workflows or comparison with existing clinical decision support systems.

Explainable AI (XAI) techniques (Grad-CAM++ and LIME) were used to highlight lesion areas, but the clinical plausibility of these heatmaps was not quantitatively validated against ground-truth annotations by dermatologists.

The preprocessing pipeline included handling of missing or corrupted images via reconstruction methods, but the specific criteria for data inclusion/exclusion and the impact of this cleaning on the final dataset distribution are not fully detailed.

The study compares optimization techniques (BFO, PCA, LDA) but does not compare the proposed hybrid framework against state-of-the-art end-to-end deep learning models or recent ensemble methods that might achieve comparable or superior performance without feature engineering.
